text: Concurrent strength and endurance training produces smaller hypertrophy gains (ES 0.85) compared to strength training alone (ES 1.23), but significantly greater than endurance training alone (ES 0.27).
specific_value: ES 0.85 concurrent vs 1.23 strength-only vs 0.27 endurance-only

text: Concurrent training produces smaller power development (ES 0.55) than strength training alone (ES 0.91), with significant differences between all three modalities including endurance only (ES 0.11).
specific_value: ES 0.55 concurrent vs 0.91 strength-only vs 0.11 endurance-only

text: Running as the endurance modality causes significant decrements in both hypertrophy and strength when combined with resistance training, whereas cycling does not.
Higher frequency of endurance training is negatively correlated with hypertrophy, strength, and power outcomes when combined with resistance training (r = -0.26 to -0.35).
Longer duration of endurance training sessions is negatively correlated with hypertrophy, strength, and power outcomes in concurrent training (r = -0.29 to -0.75).
Higher exercise intensity (% maximal heart rate) during endurance training is significantly correlated with greater body fat reduction in concurrent training programs (r = -0.60).

Interference effects of endurance training on strength outcomes are modulated by modality, frequency, and duration — runners should minimize session duration and weekly frequency of endurance work when prioritizing strength and power.
